app/oss-licenses.json:974:    "licenseText": "<p align=\"center\">\n  <a href=\"https://shittycodingagent.ai\">\n    <img src=\"https://shittycodingagent.ai/logo.svg\" alt=\"pi logo\" width=\"128\">\n  </a>\n</p>\n<p align=\"center\">\n  <a href=\"https://discord.com/invite/3cU7Bz4UPx\"><img alt=\"Discord\" src=\"https://img.shields.io/badge/discord-community-5865F2?style=flat-square&logo=discord&logoColor=white\" /></a>\n  <a href=\"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent\"><img alt=\"npm\" src=\"https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent?style=flat-square\" /></a>\n  <a href=\"https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/actions/workflows/ci.yml\"><img alt=\"Build status\" src=\"https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/badlogic/pi-mono/ci.yml?style=flat-square&branch=main\" /></a>\n</p>\n<p align=\"center\">\n  <a href=\"https://pi.dev\">pi.dev</a> domain graciously donated by\n  <br /><br />\n  <a href=\"https://exe.dev\"><img src=\"docs/images/exy.png\" alt=\"Exy mascot\" width=\"48\" /><br />exe.dev</a>\n</p>\n\nPi is a minimal terminal coding harness. Adapt pi to your workflows, not the other way around, without having to fork and modify pi internals. Extend it with TypeScript [Extensions](#extensions), [Skills](#skills), [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates), and [Themes](#themes). Put your extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes in [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) and share them with others via npm or git.\n\nPi ships with powerful defaults but skips features like sub agents and plan mode. Instead, you can ask pi to build what you want or install a third party pi package that matches your workflow.\n\nPi runs in four modes: interactive, print or JSON, RPC for process integration, and an SDK for embedding in your own apps. See [openclaw/openclaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) for a real-world SDK integration.\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n- [Quick Start](#quick-start)\n- [Providers & Models](#providers--models)\n- [Interactive Mode](#interactive-mode)\n  - [Editor](#editor)\n  - [Commands](#commands)\n  - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts)\n  - [Message Queue](#message-queue)\n- [Sessions](#sessions)\n  - [Branching](#branching)\n  - [Compaction](#compaction)\n- [Settings](#settings)\n- [Context Files](#context-files)\n- [Customization](#customization)\n  - [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates)\n  - [Skills](#skills)\n  - [Extensions](#extensions)\n  - [Themes](#themes)\n  - [Pi Packages](#pi-packages)\n- [Programmatic Usage](#programmatic-usage)\n- [Philosophy](#philosophy)\n- [CLI Reference](#cli-reference)\n\n---\n\n## Quick Start\n\n```bash\nnpm install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent\n```\n\nAuthenticate with an API key:\n\n```bash\nexport ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...\npi\n```\n\nOr use your existing subscription:\n\n```bash\npi\n/login  # Then select provider\n```\n\nThen just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via [skills](#skills), [prompt templates](#prompt-templates), [extensions](#extensions), or [pi packages](#pi-packages).\n\n**Platform notes:** [Windows](docs/windows.md) | [Termux (Android)](docs/termux.md) | [tmux](docs/tmux.md) | [Terminal setup](docs/terminal-setup.md) | [Shell aliases](docs/shell-aliases.md)\n\n---\n\n## Providers & Models\n\nFor each built-in provider, pi maintains a list of tool-capable models, updated with every release. Authenticate via subscription (`/login`) or API key, then select any model from that provider via `/model` (or Ctrl+L).\n\n**Subscriptions:**\n- Anthropic Claude Pro/Max\n- OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro (Codex)\n- GitHub Copilot\n- Google Gemini CLI\n- Google Antigravity\n\n**API keys:**\n- Anthropic\n- OpenAI\n- Azure OpenAI\n- Google Gemini\n- Google Vertex\n- Amazon Bedrock\n- Mistral\n- Groq\n- Cerebras\n- xAI\n- OpenRouter\n- Vercel AI Gateway\n- ZAI\n- OpenCode Zen\n- OpenCode Go\n- Hugging Face\n- Kimi For Coding\n- MiniMax\n\nSee [docs/providers.md](docs/providers.md) for detailed setup instructions.\n\n**Custom providers & models:** Add providers via `~/.pi/agent/models.json` if they speak a supported API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). For custom APIs or OAuth, use extensions. See [docs/models.md](docs/models.md) and [docs/custom-provider.md](docs/custom-provider.md).\n\n---\n\n## Interactive Mode\n\n<p align=\"center\"><img src=\"docs/images/interactive-mode.png\" alt=\"Interactive Mode\" width=\"600\"></p>\n\nThe interface from top to bottom:\n\n- **Startup header** - Shows shortcuts (`/hotkeys` for all), loaded AGENTS.md files, prompt templates, skills, and extensions\n- **Messages** - Your messages, assistant responses, tool calls and results, notifications, errors, and extension UI\n- **Editor** - Where you type; border color indicates thinking level\n- **Footer** - Working directory, session name, total token/cache usage, cost, context usage, current model\n\nThe editor can be temporarily replaced by other UI, like built-in `/settings` or custom UI from extensions (e.g., a Q&A tool that lets the user answer model questions in a structured format). [Extensions](#extensions) can also replace the editor, add widgets above/below it, a status line, custom footer, or overlays.\n\n### Editor\n\n| Feature | How |\n|---------|-----|\n| File reference | Type `@` to fuzzy-search project files |\n| Path completion | Tab to complete paths |\n| Multi-line | Shift+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal) |\n| Images | Ctrl+V to paste (Alt+V on Windows), or drag onto terminal |\n| Bash commands | `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending |\n\nStandard editing keybindings for delete word, undo, etc. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md).\n\n### Commands\n\nType `/` in the editor to trigger commands. [Extensions](#extensions) can register custom commands, [skills](#skills) are available as `/skill:name`, and [prompt templates](#prompt-templates) expand via `/templatename`.\n\n| Command | Description |\n|---------|-------------|\n| `/login`, `/logout` | OAuth authentication |\n| `/model` | Switch models |\n| `/scoped-models` | Enable/disable models for Ctrl+P cycling |\n| `/settings` | Thinking level, theme, message delivery, transport |\n| `/resume` | Pick from previous sessions |\n| `/new` | Start a new session |\n| `/name <name>` | Set session display name |\n| `/session` | Show session info (path, tokens, cost) |\n| `/tree` | Jump to any point in the session and continue from there |\n| `/fork` | Create a new session from the current branch |\n| `/compact [prompt]` | Manually compact context, optional custom instructions |\n| `/copy` | Copy last assistant message to clipboard |\n| `/export [file]` | Export session to HTML file |\n| `/share` | Upload as private GitHub gist with shareable HTML link |\n| `/reload` | Reload extensions, skills, prompts, context files (themes hot-reload automatically) |\n| `/hotkeys` | Show all keyboard shortcuts |\n| `/changelog` | Display version history |\n| `/quit`, `/exit` | Quit pi |\n\n### Keyboard Shortcuts\n\nSee `/hotkeys` for the full list. Customize via `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md).\n\n**Commonly used:**\n\n| Key | Action |\n|-----|--------|\n| Ctrl+C | Clear editor |\n| Ctrl+C twice | Quit |\n| Escape | Cancel/abort |\n| Escape twice | Open `/tree` |\n| Ctrl+L | Open model selector |\n| Ctrl+P / Shift+Ctrl+P | Cycle scoped models forward/backward |\n| Shift+Tab | Cycle thinking level |\n| Ctrl+O | Collapse/expand tool output |\n| Ctrl+T | Collapse/expand thinking blocks |\n\n### Message Queue\n\nSubmit messages while the agent is working:\n\n- **Enter** queues a *steering* message, delivered after current tool execution (interrupts remaining tools)\n- **Alt+Enter** queues a *follow-up* message, delivered only after the agent finishes all work\n- **Escape** aborts and restores queued messages to editor\n- **Alt+Up** retrieves queued messages back to editor\n\nConfigure delivery in [settings](docs/settings.md): `steeringMode` and `followUpMode` can be `\"one-at-a-time\"` (default, waits for response) or `\"all\"` (delivers all queued at once). `transport` selects provider transport preference (`\"sse\"`, `\"websocket\"`, or `\"auto\"`) for providers that support multiple transports.\n\n---\n\n## Sessions\n\nSessions are stored as JSONL files with a tree structure. Each entry has an `id` and `parentId`, enabling in-place branching without creating new files. See [docs/session.md](docs/session.md) for file format.\n\n### Management\n\nSessions auto-save to `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` organized by working directory.\n\n```bash\npi -c                  # Continue most recent session\npi -r                  # Browse and select from past sessions\npi --no-session        # Ephemeral mode (don't save)\npi --session <path>    # Use specific session file or ID\n```\n\n### Branching\n\n**`/tree`** - Navigate the session tree in-place. Select any previous point, continue from there, and switch between branches. All history preserved in a single file.\n\n<p align=\"center\"><img src=\"docs/images/tree-view.png\" alt=\"Tree View\" width=\"600\"></p>\n\n- Search by typing, fold/unfold and jump between branches with Ctrl+←/Ctrl+→ or Alt+←/Alt+→, page with ←/→\n- Filter modes (Ctrl+O): default → no-tools → user-only → labeled-only → all\n- Press `l` to label entries as bookmarks\n\n**`/fork`** - Create a new session file from the current branch. Opens a selector, copies history up to the selected point, and places that message in the editor for modification.\n\n### Compaction\n\nLong sessions can exhaust context windows. Compaction summarizes older messages while keeping recent ones.\n\n**Manual:** `/compact` or `/compact <custom instructions>`\n\n**Automatic:** Enabled by default. Triggers on context overflow (recovers and retries) or when approaching the limit (proactive). Configure via `/settings` or `settings.json`.\n\nCompaction is lossy. The full history remains in the JSONL file; use `/tree` to revisit. Customize compaction behavior via [extensions](#extensions). See [docs/compaction.md](docs/compaction.md) for internals.\n\n---\n\n## Settings\n\nUse `/settings` to modify common options, or edit JSON files directly:\n\n| Location | Scope |\n|----------|-------|\n| `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` | Global (all projects) |\n| `.pi/settings.json` | Project (overrides global) |\n\nSee [docs/settings.md](docs/settings.md) for all options.\n\n---\n\n## Context Files\n\nPi loads `AGENTS.md` (or `CLAUDE.md`) at startup from:\n- `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` (global)\n- Parent directories (walking up from cwd)\n- Current directory\n\nUse for project instructions, conventions, common commands. All matching files are concatenated.\n\n### System Prompt\n\nReplace the default system prompt with `.pi/SYSTEM.md` (project) or `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` (global). Append without replacing via `APPEND_SYSTEM.md`.\n\n---\n\n## Customization\n\n### Prompt Templates\n\nReusable prompts as Markdown files. Type `/name` to expand.\n\n```markdown\n<!-- ~/.pi/agent/prompts/review.md -->\nReview this code for bugs, security issues, and performance problems.\nFocus on: {{focus}}\n```\n\nPlace in `~/.pi/agent/prompts/`, `.pi/prompts/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/prompt-templates.md](docs/prompt-templates.md).\n\n### Skills\n\nOn-demand capability packages following the [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io). Invoke via `/skill:name` or let the agent load them automatically.\n\n```markdown\n<!-- ~/.pi/agent/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md -->\n# My Skill\nUse this skill when the user asks about X.\n\n## Steps\n1. Do this\n2. Then that\n```\n\nPlace in `~/.pi/agent/skills/`, `~/.agents/skills/`, `.pi/skills/`, or `.agents/skills/` (from `cwd` up through parent directories) or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/skills.md](docs/skills.md).\n\n### Extensions\n\n<p align=\"center\"><img src=\"docs/images/doom-extension.png\" alt=\"Doom Extension\" width=\"600\"></p>\n\nTypeScript modules that extend pi with custom tools, commands, keyboard shortcuts, event handlers, and UI components.\n\n```typescript\nexport default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {\n  pi.registerTool({ name: \"deploy\", ... });\n  pi.registerCommand(\"stats\", { ... });\n  pi.on(\"tool_call\", async (event, ctx) => { ... });\n}\n```\n\n**What's possible:**\n- Custom tools (or replace built-in tools entirely)\n- Sub-agents and plan mode\n- Custom compaction and summarization\n- Permission gates and path protection\n- Custom editors and UI components\n- Status lines, headers, footers\n- Git checkpointing and auto-commit\n- SSH and sandbox execution\n- MCP server integration\n- Make pi look like Claude Code\n- Games while waiting (yes, Doom runs)\n- ...anything you can dream up\n\nPlace in `~/.pi/agent/extensions/`, `.pi/extensions/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/extensions.md](docs/extensions.md) and [examples/extensions/](examples/extensions/).\n\n### Themes\n\nBuilt-in: `dark`, `light`. Themes hot-reload: modify the active theme file and pi immediately applies changes.\n\nPlace in `~/.pi/agent/themes/`, `.pi/themes/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/themes.md](docs/themes.md).\n\n### Pi Packages\n\nBundle and share extensions, skills, prompts, and themes via npm or git. Find packages on [npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com/search?q=keywords%3Api-package) or [Discord](https://discord.com/channels/1456806362351669492/1457744485428629628).\n\n> **Security:** Pi packages run with full system access. Extensions execute arbitrary code, and skills can instruct the model to perform any action including running executables. Review source code before installing third-party packages.\n\n```bash\npi install npm:@foo/pi-tools\npi install npm:@foo/pi-tools@1.2.3      # pinned version\npi install git:github.com/user/repo\npi install git:github.com/user/repo@v1  # tag or commit\npi install git:git@github.com:user/repo\npi install git:git@github.com:user/repo@v1  # tag or commit\npi install https://github.com/user/repo\npi install https://github.com/user/repo@v1      # tag or commit\npi install ssh://git@github.com/user/repo\npi install ssh://git@github.com/user/repo@v1    # tag or commit\npi remove npm:@foo/pi-tools\npi list\npi update                               # skips pinned packages\npi config                               # enable/disable extensions, skills, prompts, themes\n```\n\nPackages install to `~/.pi/agent/git/` (git) or global npm. Use `-l` for project-local installs (`.pi/git/`, `.pi/npm/`).\n\nCreate a package by adding a `pi` key to `package.json`:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"name\": \"my-pi-package\",\n  \"keywords\": [\"pi-package\"],\n  \"pi\": {\n    \"extensions\": [\"./extensions\"],\n    \"skills\": [\"./skills\"],\n    \"prompts\": [\"./prompts\"],\n    \"themes\": [\"./themes\"]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWithout a `pi` manifest, pi auto-discovers from conventional directories (`extensions/`, `skills/`, `prompts/`, `themes/`).\n\nSee [docs/packages.md](docs/packages.md).\n\n---\n\n## Programmatic Usage\n\n### SDK\n\n```typescript\nimport { AuthStorage, createAgentSession, ModelRegistry, SessionManager } from \"@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent\";\n\nconst { session } = await createAgentSession({\n  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),\n  authStorage: AuthStorage.create(),\n  modelRegistry: new ModelRegistry(authStorage),\n});\n\nawait session.prompt(\"What files are in the current directory?\");\n```\n\nSee [docs/sdk.md](docs/sdk.md) and [examples/sdk/](examples/sdk/).\n\n### RPC Mode\n\nFor non-Node.js integrations, use RPC mode over stdin/stdout:\n\n```bash\npi --mode rpc\n```\n\nRPC mode uses strict LF-delimited JSONL framing. Clients must split records on `\\n` only. Do not use generic line readers like Node `readline`, which also split on Unicode separators inside JSON payloads.\n\nSee [docs/rpc.md](docs/rpc.md) for the protocol.\n\n---\n\n## Philosophy\n\nPi is aggressively extensible so it doesn't have to dictate your workflow. Features that other tools bake in can be built with [extensions](#extensions), [skills](#skills), or installed from third-party [pi packages](#pi-packages). This keeps the core minimal while letting you shape pi to fit how you work.\n\n**No MCP.** Build CLI tools with READMEs (see [Skills](#skills)), or build an extension that adds MCP support. [Why?](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-02-what-if-you-dont-need-mcp/)\n\n**No sub-agents.** There's many ways to do this. Spawn pi instances via tmux, or build your own with [extensions](#extensions), or install a package that does it your way.\n\n**No permission popups.** Run in a container, or build your own confirmation flow with [extensions](#extensions) inline with your environment and security requirements.\n\n**No plan mode.** Write plans to files, or build it with [extensions](#extensions), or install a package.\n\n**No built-in to-dos.** They confuse models. Use a TODO.md file, or build your own with [extensions](#extensions).\n\n**No background bash.** Use tmux. Full observability, direct interaction.\n\nRead the [blog post](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-30-pi-coding-agent/) for the full rationale.\n\n---\n\n## CLI Reference\n\n```bash\npi [options] [@files...] [messages...]\n```\n\n### Package Commands\n\n```bash\npi install <source> [-l]    # Install package, -l for project-local\npi remove <source> [-l]     # Remove package\npi update [source]          # Update packages (skips pinned)\npi list                     # List installed packages\npi config                   # Enable/disable package resources\n```\n\n### Modes\n\n| Flag | Description |\n|------|-------------|\n| (default) | Interactive mode |\n| `-p`, `--print` | Print response and exit |\n| `--mode json` | Output all events as JSON lines (see [docs/json.md](docs/json.md)) |\n| `--mode rpc` | RPC mode for process integration (see [docs/rpc.md](docs/rpc.md)) |\n| `--export <in> [out]` | Export session to HTML |\n\n### Model Options\n\n| Option | Description |\n|--------|-------------|\n| `--provider <name>` | Provider (anthropic, openai, google, etc.) |\n| `--model <pattern>` | Model pattern or ID (supports `provider/id` and optional `:<thinking>`) |\n| `--api-key <key>` | API key (overrides env vars) |\n| `--thinking <level>` | `off`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh` |\n| `--models <patterns>` | Comma-separated patterns for Ctrl+P cycling |\n| `--list-models [search]` | List available models |\n\n### Session Options\n\n| Option | Description |\n|--------|-------------|\n| `-c`, `--continue` | Continue most recent session |\n| `-r`, `--resume` | Browse and select session |\n| `--session <path>` | Use specific session file or partial UUID |\n| `--session-dir <dir>` | Custom session storage directory |\n| `--no-session` | Ephemeral mode (don't save) |\n\n### Tool Options\n\n| Option | Description |\n|--------|-------------|\n| `--tools <list>` | Enable specific built-in tools (default: `read,bash,edit,write`) |\n| `--no-tools` | Disable all built-in tools (extension tools still work) |\n\nAvailable built-in tools: `read`, `bash`, `edit`, `write`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`\n\n### Resource Options\n\n| Option | Description |\n|--------|-------------|\n| `-e`, `--extension <source>` | Load extension from path, npm, or git (repeatable) |\n| `--no-extensions` | Disable extension discovery |\n| `--skill <path>` | Load skill (repeatable) |\n| `--no-skills` | Disable skill discovery |\n| `--prompt-template <path>` | Load prompt template (repeatable) |\n| `--no-prompt-templates` | Disable prompt template discovery |\n| `--theme <path>` | Load theme (repeatable) |\n| `--no-themes` | Disable theme discovery |\n\nCombine `--no-*` with explicit flags to load exactly what you need, ignoring settings.json (e.g., `--no-extensions -e ./my-ext.ts`).\n\n### Other Options\n\n| Option | Description |\n|--------|-------------|\n| `--system-prompt <text>` | Replace default prompt (context files and skills still appended) |\n| `--append-system-prompt <text>` | Append to system prompt |\n| `--verbose` | Force verbose startup |\n| `-h`, `--help` | Show help |\n| `-v`, `--version` | Show version |\n\n### File Arguments\n\nPrefix files with `@` to include in the message:\n\n```bash\npi @prompt.md \"Answer this\"\npi -p @screenshot.png \"What's in this image?\"\npi @code.ts @test.ts \"Review these files\"\n```\n\n### Examples\n\n```bash\n# Interactive with initial prompt\npi \"List all .ts files in src/\"\n\n# Non-interactive\npi -p \"Summarize this codebase\"\n\n# Different model\npi --provider openai --model gpt-4o \"Help me refactor\"\n\n# Model with provider prefix (no --provider needed)\npi --model openai/gpt-4o \"Help me refactor\"\n\n# Model with thinking level shorthand\npi --model sonnet:high \"Solve this complex problem\"\n\n# Limit model cycling\npi --models \"claude-*,gpt-4o\"\n\n# Read-only mode\npi --tools read,grep,find,ls -p \"Review the code\"\n\n# High thinking level\npi --thinking high \"Solve this complex problem\"\n```\n\n### Environment Variables\n\n| Variable | Description |\n|----------|-------------|\n| `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR` | Override config directory (default: `~/.pi/agent`) |\n| `PI_PACKAGE_DIR` | Override package directory (useful for Nix/Guix where store paths tokenize poorly) |\n| `PI_SKIP_VERSION_CHECK` | Skip version check at startup |\n| `PI_CACHE_RETENTION` | Set to `long` for extended prompt cache (Anthropic: 1h, OpenAI: 24h) |\n| `VISUAL`, `EDITOR` | External editor for Ctrl+G |\n\n---\n\n## Contributing & Development\n\nSee [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines and [docs/development.md](docs/development.md) for setup, forking, and debugging.\n\n---\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n\n## See framework\n- [@mariozechner/pi-tui](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-tui): Terminal UI components\n",

By contrast,\nthe GNU General Public License is intended to guarantee your freedom to\nshare and change all versions of a program--to make sure it remains free\nsoftware for all its users.  We, the Free Software Foundation, use the\nGNU General Public License for most of our software; it applies also to\nany other work released this way by its authors.  You can apply it to\nyour programs, too.\n\n  When we speak of free software, we are referring to freedom, not\nprice.  Our General Public Licenses are designed to make sure that you\nhave the freedom to distribute copies of free software (and charge for\nthem if you wish), that you receive source code or can get it if you\nwant it, that you can change the software or use pieces of it in new\nfree programs, and that you know you can do these things.\n\n  To protect your rights, we need to prevent others from denying you\nthese rights or asking you to surrender the rights.  Therefore, you have\ncertain responsibilities if you distribute copies of the software, or if\nyou modify it: responsibilities to respect the freedom of others.\n\n  For example, if you distribute copies of such a program, whether\ngratis or for a fee, you must pass on to the recipients the same\nfreedoms that you received.  You must make sure that they, too, receive\nor can get the source code.  And you must show them these terms so they\nknow their rights.\n\n  Developers that use the GNU GPL protect your rights with two steps:\n(1) assert copyright on the software, and (2) offer you this License\ngiving you legal permission to copy, distribute and/or modify it.\n\n  For the developers' and authors' protection, the GPL clearly explains\nthat there is no warranty for this free software.  For both users' and\nauthors' sake, the GPL requires that modified versions be marked as\nchanged, so that their problems will not be attributed erroneously to\nauthors of previous versions.\n\n  Some devices are designed to deny users access to install or run\nmodified versions of the software inside them, although the manufacturer\ncan do so.  This is fundamentally incompatible with the aim of\nprotecting users' freedom to change the software.  The systematic\npattern of such abuse occurs in the area of products for individuals to\nuse, which is precisely where it is most unacceptable.  Therefore, we\nhave designed this version of the GPL to prohibit the practice for those\nproducts.  If such problems arise substantially in other domains, we\nstand ready to extend this provision to those domains in future versions\nof the GPL, as needed to protect the freedom of users.\n\n  Finally, every program is threatened constantly by software patents.\nStates should not allow patents to restrict development and use of\nsoftware on general-purpose computers, but in those that do, we wish to\navoid the special danger that patents applied to a free program could\nmake it effectively proprietary.  To prevent this, the GPL assures that\npatents cannot be used to render the program non-free.\n\n  The precise terms and conditions for copying, distribution and\nmodification follow.\n\n                       TERMS AND CONDITIONS\n\n  0. Definitions.\n\n  \"This License\" refers to version 3 of the GNU General Public License.\n\n  \"Copyright\" also means copyright-like laws that apply to other kinds of\nworks, such as semiconductor masks.\n\n  \"The Program\" refers to any copyrightable work licensed under this\nLicense. The resulting work is called a \"modified version\" of the\nearlier work or a work \"based on\" the earlier work.\n\n  A \"covered work\" means either the unmodified Program or a work based\non the Program.\n\n  To \"propagate\" a work means to do anything with it that, without\npermission, would make you directly or secondarily liable for\ninfringement under applicable copyright law, except executing it on a\ncomputer or modifying a private copy. By contrast,\nthe GNU General Public License is intended to guarantee your freedom to\nshare and change all versions of a program--to make sure it remains free\nsoftware for all its users.  We, the Free Software Foundation, use the\nGNU General Public License for most of our software; it applies also to\nany other work released this way by its authors.  You can apply it to\nyour programs, too.\n\n  When we speak of free software, we are referring to freedom, not\nprice.  Our General Public Licenses are designed to make sure that you\nhave the freedom to distribute copies of free software (and charge for\nthem if you wish), that you receive source code or can get it if you\nwant it, that you can change the software or use pieces of it in new\nfree programs, and that you know you can do these things.\n\n  To protect your rights, we need to prevent others from denying you\nthese rights or asking you to surrender the rights.  Therefore, you have\ncertain responsibilities if you distribute copies of the software, or if\nyou modify it: responsibilities to respect the freedom of others.\n\n  For example, if you distribute copies of such a program, whether\ngratis or for a fee, you must pass on to the recipients the same\nfreedoms that you received.  You must make sure that they, too, receive\nor can get the source code.  And you must show them these terms so they\nknow their rights.\n\n  Developers that use the GNU GPL protect your rights with two steps:\n(1) assert copyright on the software, and (2) offer you this License\ngiving you legal permission to copy, distribute and/or modify it.\n\n  For the developers' and authors' protection, the GPL clearly explains\nthat there is no warranty for this free software.  For both users' and\nauthors' sake, the GPL requires that modified versions be marked as\nchanged, so that their problems will not be attributed erroneously to\nauthors of previous versions.\n\n  Some devices are designed to deny users access to install or run\nmodified versions of the software inside them, although the manufacturer\ncan do so.  This is fundamentally incompatible with the aim of\nprotecting users' freedom to change the software.  The systematic\npattern of such abuse occurs in the area of products for individuals to\nuse, which is precisely where it is most unacceptable.  Therefore, we\nhave designed this version of the GPL to prohibit the practice for those\nproducts.  If such problems arise substantially in other domains, we\nstand ready to extend this provision to those domains in future versions\nof the GPL, as needed to protect the freedom of users.\n\n  Finally, every program is threatened constantly by software patents.\nStates should not allow patents to restrict development and use of\nsoftware on general-purpose computers, but in those that do, we wish to\navoid the special danger that patents applied to a free program could\nmake it effectively proprietary.  To prevent this, the GPL assures that\npatents cannot be used to render the program non-free.\n\n  The precise terms and conditions for copying, distribution and\nmodification follow.\n\n                       TERMS AND CONDITIONS\n\n  0.  succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# Plow App
     2	
     3	The Plow macOS app is currently a local OpenClaw wrapper/orchestrator centered on **PlowD** (the daemon).
     4	
     5	## Current Focus
     6	
     7	As of this branch, the primary path is:
     8	
     9	- PlowD starts and manages OpenClaw gateway + node host locally.
    10	- Plow app tooling keeps app-managed config/state under `~/Library/Application Support/co.plow.app/...` instead of `~/.openclaw/...`.
    11	- Plow gateway policy/operator logic handles pairing and tool approval flows.
    12	
    13	The older Wren/Inbox agent-first flow is not the main focus right now, and agent registration on daemon startup is currently disabled.
    14	
    15	## Architecture (Now)
    16	
    17	- **PlowD (FastAPI daemon)**: process manager + policy service + status API.
    18	- **OpenClaw gateway**: local websocket gateway (`127.0.0.1`, token auth).
    19	- **OpenClaw node host**: execution host process paired to the gateway.
    20	- **OpenClaw dashboard/web chat**: optional local UI client that connects to the same gateway.
    21	- **Plow macOS app**: still exists as iMessage bridge/UI layer, but not the center of this PR.
    22	
    23	OpenClaw terms vs app terms:
    24	
    25	- PlowD == daemon/operator-side process (not the gateway binary itself)
    26	- OpenClaw gateway == websocket service PlowD launches/manages
    27	- Web client/dashboard == separate operator client that can connect locally when paired/authenticated
    28	
    29	State roots are bundle-scoped:
    30	
    31	- production app: `~/Library/Application Support/co.plow.app/`
    32	- default dev app: `~/Library/Application Support/co.plow.app.dev/`
    33	- worktree/dev variants: `~/Library/Application Support/<bundle-id>/`
    34	
    35	## Quick Start
    36	
    37	For the full local stack, use the root [README.md](../README.md). This document stays focused on app-specific details.
    38	
    39	1. Set up daemon env:
    40	
    41	```bash
    42	cd app/plowd
    43	uv sync
    44	cp .env.example .env
    45	```
    46	
    47	2. Fill `app/plowd/.env` with at least:
    48	
    49	```env
    50	PLOW_API_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8000
    51	```
    52	
    53	3. Build the app bundle and packaged runtime:
    54	
    55	```bash
    56	just app build
    57	```
    58	
    59	4. Run Plow against the local API:
    60	
    95	- use `app/justfile` only for local Swift app commands
    96	- use `app/plowd/justfile` only for local daemon tests and internal maintenance; the supported app workflows are the `just app ...` commands above
    97	
    98	## Code Signing
    99	
   100	Local app builds are signed by default with the shared Apple team configured in [`app/Phoenix/Config/App.xcconfig`](../app/Phoenix/Config/App.xcconfig). That keeps dev builds closer to production identity and is the preferred path for avoiding repeated Full Disk Access prompts during normal rebuilds.
   101	
   102	For release notarization setup and the personal-team override path, see [`docs/distribution/CODE_SIGNING.md`](distribution/CODE_SIGNING.md).
   103	
   104	## State Boundaries (Avoiding Sprawl)
   105	
   106	OpenClaw runs inside a VM sandbox managed by PlowD. All state is pinned to app-managed paths under `~/Library/Application Support/<bundle-id>/`:
   107	
   108	- config: `~/Library/Application Support/<bundle-id>/openclaw/gateway/openclaw.json`
   109	- state: `~/Library/Application Support/<bundle-id>/openclaw/gateway`
   110	- token: `~/Library/Application Support/<bundle-id>/openclaw/secrets/gateway-token`
   111	
   112	Reference and recovery notes:
   113	
   114	- [plowd/README.md](../app/plowd/README.md)
   115	- [openclaw-phoenixd-known-behaviors.md](openclaw-phoenixd-known-behaviors.md)

     1	# Plow
     2	
     3	Plow is an agent platform that gives OpenClaw agents narrow, permission-gated access to cloud services (Gmail, Slack) and on-device capabilities through a local approval layer.
     4	
     5	The system has three tiers:
     6	
     7	- **Plow API** (`api/`) — cloud service that owns connector OAuth, opportunity matching, marketplace skills, and policy enforcement
     8	- **Plow app** (`app/`) — macOS app (Swift) + plowd daemon (Python) that manages on-device approval, gateway configuration, and VM lifecycle
     9	- **Plow CLI** (`cli/`) — narrow tool surface inside the OpenClaw container, talks directly to the Plow API
    10	
    11	## Architecture
    12	
    13	```mermaid
    14	flowchart TB
    15	    User[User]
    16	
    17	    subgraph Mac["macOS Host"]
    18	        Phoenix["Plow.app\n(Swift · Virtualization.framework)"]
    19	        Plowd["plowd daemon\n(:18788 · Python FastAPI)"]
    20	
    21	        subgraph VM["Alpine Linux VM"]
    22	            GW["OpenClaw Gateway\n(:18789)"]
    23	            Chan["plow-imessage channel"]
    24	            OC["OpenClaw Agent"]
    25	            CLI["plow CLI\n(containercli)"]
    26	        end
    27	    end
    28	
    29	    subgraph Cloud["Cloud (AWS ECS)"]
    30	        API["Plow API\n(FastAPI)"]
    31	        DB["PostgreSQL\n+ pgvector"]
    32	        OE["Opportunity Engine\n(LLM-based JTBD matching)"]
    33	
    34	        subgraph Connectors
    35	            GM[Gmail]
    36	            SL[Slack]
    37	        end
    38	
    39	        subgraph Channels
    40	            LQ[Linq]
    41	        end
    42	    end
    43	
    44	    User --> Phoenix
    45	    Phoenix -->|vsock| VM

 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# Phoenix + OpenClaw Security Architecture (Current State and Hardening Roadmap)
     2	
     3	Last updated: 2026-03-03
     4	
     5	## Purpose
     6	
     7	This document describes:
     8	1. The architecture Phoenix is running today.
     9	2. What security guarantees exist right now.
    10	3. What is not guaranteed yet.
    11	4. A phased hardening roadmap.
    12	
    13	This is a living architecture document for implementation and product/security decisions.
    14	
    15	## System Overview (Current)
    16	
    17	Core components:
    18	1. `Phoenix.app` (macOS UI client).
    19	2. `phoenixd` (local policy and orchestration daemon).
    20	3. `openclaw-gateway` (tool runtime and agent loop, `exec.host = "gateway"` inside Alpine VM).
    21	4. `phoenix-gateway-guard` OpenClaw plugin (`before_tool_call` / `after_tool_call` hooks).
    22	
    23	Current runtime shape:
    24	1. Gateway is bound to loopback (`127.0.0.1`) with token auth.
    25	2. PhoenixD manages OpenClaw config reconciliation and process supervision.
    26	3. PhoenixD resolves exec approval events over gateway operator websocket.
    27	4. Tool policy decisions are requested from PhoenixD by plugin hook callbacks.
    28	
    29	## Trust Boundaries (Current)
    30	
    31	1. UI does not need direct gateway token by design.
    32	2. Gateway token is stored in user home (`~/.phoenix/openclaw/secrets/gateway-token`) with mode `0600`.
    33	3. Gateway/API access is local-only by bind setting, but same-user processes remain in the trust boundary.
    34	4. OpenClaw runtime (gateway + node host) is currently trusted to honor plugin/approval contracts.
    35	
    94	### Phase 0: Baseline discipline (now)
    95	
    96	1. Keep loopback bind and token auth mandatory.
    97	2. Keep plugin required and fail-closed for policy check.
    98	3. Keep exec ask/approval as deny-by-default fallback.
    99	4. Keep OpenClaw version pinning and deterministic config reconciliation.
   100	5. Prefer policy mode `ask` after approval UI is live.
   101	
   102	### Phase 1: UX and approval authority hardening
   103	
   104	1. Add PhoenixD <-> app websocket channel for real-time approvals.
   105	2. Move default approval mode from auto-allow to ask.
   106	3. Improve retry behavior around first exec/pairing race.
   107	4. Add token rotation command and operational runbook.
   108	5. Add explicit principal mapping and mismatch-deny logic keyed by `(nodeId, sessionKey)`.
   109	
   110	### Phase 2: Packaging and deterministic runtime
   111	
   112	1. Bundle pinned Node + OpenClaw runtime in signed installer payload.

From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Apr 2026 14:55:21 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 02/12] fix(phoenix): revert KeepMacAwake toggle when assertion
 acquire fails

If IOPMAssertionCreateWithName returns non-success, isEnabled is now
flipped back to false (and persisted) so the UI no longer shows a
"keep awake" state that the kernel didn't grant. didSet recursion is
gated by a `suppressApply` flag so the revert can't loop.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

-    private func acquireAssertion() {
-        guard !hasAssertion else { return }
+    /// Brings the held IOPMAssertion in line with `isEnabled` and persists the
+    /// resolved value. If acquire fails, `isEnabled` is reverted to false so
+    /// the toggle UI matches the actual sleep-prevention state.
+    private func applyEnabled() {
+        guard !suppressApply else { return }
+        if isEnabled {
+            if !acquireAssertion() {
+                suppressApply = true
+                isEnabled = false
+                suppressApply = false
+            }
+        } else {
+            releaseAssertion()
+        }
+        UserDefaults.standard.set(isEnabled, forKey: Self.preferencesKey)
+    }
+
+    private func acquireAssertion() -> Bool {
+        guard !hasAssertion else { return true }
         var id: IOPMAssertionID = 0
         let reason = "Plow keeping Mac awake so the OpenClaw VM stays reachable" as CFString
         let result = IOPMAssertionCreateWithName(

         }
         assertionID = id
         hasAssertion = true
+        return true
     }

From c06d059ebde64258a8c22cc40f38b7730d22e2a3 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Apr 2026 14:55:29 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 03/12] fix(phoenix): auto-size installer window to settings
 content

Replace the hard-coded 530/610 window heights with a SwiftUI preference
key (InstallerContentHeightKey) that propagates SettingsView's measured
content height up to InstallerView. The hosting NSWindow resizes to fit
that height, capped by visibleFrame - 80 so the existing ScrollView
still acts as a fallback when content genuinely exceeds the screen.

Splash screens (welcomeInstall, FDA, activation, connectContext) keep
the design-set 530 since their layouts rely on Spacer fill, not
intrinsic content height.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
---

 app/Phoenix/SettingsView.swift  |  8 +++++++

+        .onPreferenceChange(InstallerContentHeightKey.self) { newHeight in
+            measuredContentHeight = newHeight
+        }
+        .onChange(of: measuredContentHeight) {
+            updateWindowHeight(for: screen)
+        }
         .onDisappear {
             connectorPollTimer?.invalidate()
             connectorPollTimer = nil
@@ -487,7 +496,7 @@ struct InstallerView: View {
 
     private func updateWindowHeight(for screen: InstallerScreen) {
         guard let window = NSApp.windows.first(where: { $0.identifier?.rawValue == "installer" }) else { return }
-        let targetHeight: CGFloat = screen == .connectors ? 610 : 530
+        let targetHeight = targetWindowHeight(for: screen)
         let currentFrame = window.frame
         guard abs(currentFrame.height - targetHeight) > 1 else { return }
         let newOriginY = currentFrame.origin.y + (currentFrame.height - targetHeight)
@@ -503,6 +512,34 @@ struct InstallerView: View {
             window.animator().setFrame(newFrame, display: true)
         }
     }
+
+    /// The connectors screen sizes its window to the measured content height
+    /// (reported via `InstallerContentHeightKey`), capped by the visible
+    /// screen so a long settings list falls back to scrolling inside the
+    /// existing `ScrollView` instead of pushing the window off-screen. The
+    /// remaining screens are intentionally taller than their content
+    /// (Spacer-filled splash layouts) and keep the design height of 530.
+    private func targetWindowHeight(for screen: InstallerScreen) -> CGFloat {
+        let cap = max(420, (NSScreen.main?.visibleFrame.height ?? 900) - 80)
+        if screen == .connectors, measuredContentHeight > 0 {
+            // `measuredContentHeight` is the ScrollView's inner content; add
+            // the window's titlebar so the visible area matches.
+            return min(measuredContentHeight + 28, cap)
+        }
+        return 530
+    }
+}
+
+/// SwiftUI preference key carrying the measured intrinsic height of the
+/// active installer screen up to `InstallerView`, which drives the hosting
+/// `NSWindow`'s frame. Emitted by `SettingsView` (the only screen whose
+/// content can grow as connectors/containers come and go); unspecified for
+/// the splash screens, which use a design-set fixed height.
+struct InstallerContentHeightKey: PreferenceKey {
+    static var defaultValue: CGFloat = 0
+    static func reduce(value: inout CGFloat, nextValue: () -> CGFloat) {
+        value = nextValue()
+    }
 }
 
 struct InstallPrimaryButtonStyle: ButtonStyle {

             .padding(.top, 40)
             .padding(.bottom, 50)

+                GeometryReader { geo in

+                        key: InstallerContentHeightKey.self,

From ad4e121fc43e8f99402f48a7bdace8623b498e24 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Apr 2026 15:09:05 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 04/12] fix(phoenix): include DownloadBarView height in
 installer auto-size

The bottom-anchored DownloadBarView is a ZStack overlay, so SettingsView's
ScrollView didn't know about it; when settings content fit, the last rows
sat hidden behind the bar (worse in the retry state, where the bar grows).

Add a second preference key (InstallerOverlayHeightKey) that reports the
bar's measured height to InstallerView. targetWindowHeight now adds it to
the connectors-screen target so the bar always sits below the content.
The key reverts to 0 when the bar leaves the view tree (post-install),
restoring the bar-less sizing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

+/// Carries the bottom-anchored `DownloadBarView`'s measured height up to
+/// `InstallerView` so the connectors-screen window can grow to keep the
+/// settings content above the bar (including the taller "retry" state).
+/// Reverts to zero when the bar isn't in the view tree (post-install).
+struct InstallerOverlayHeightKey: PreferenceKey {
+    static var defaultValue: CGFloat = 0
+    static func reduce(value: inout CGFloat, nextValue: () -> CGFloat) {
+        value = nextValue()
+    }
+}

From 328d02ed9dba2bad8e9a0ea673b2854deb5d95ee Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Apr 2026 18:13:16 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 09/12] test(phoenix): cover KeepMacAwake failed-acquire
 rollback

Add an injectable AssertionCreator closure (and a UserDefaults injection
point) on KeepMacAwake so a deterministic kIOReturnError can be fed in
without touching real IOKit.

KeepMacAwakeTests.testFailedAcquireRevertsToggleAndWritesOnceWithoutRecursion
asserts the round-2 contract: when acquire fails, isEnabled reverts to
false, UserDefaults persists false, the assertion creator is invoked
exactly once (no recursion through suppressApply), and exactly one
UserDefaults write of the resolved value occurs for the toggle.

The pre-fix didSet path would have left isEnabled=true and UserDefaults=
true after a failed acquire, so this test fails red against the pre-74908cac code.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
---

diff --git a/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift b/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift
index 52668392b..f299782c0 100644
--- a/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift
+++ b/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift
@@ -14,17 +14,35 @@ import Observation
 final class KeepMacAwake {
     static let preferencesKey = "keepMacAwakeWhileRunning"
 
+    /// Wraps `IOPMAssertionCreateWithName` so tests can inject deterministic
+    /// failure (kIOReturnError) without depending on real IOKit. The default
+    /// invokes the system call; tests pass a closure that returns whatever
+    /// (IOReturn, IOPMAssertionID) tuple they want.
+    typealias AssertionCreator = () -> (result: IOReturn, assertionID: IOPMAssertionID)
+
+    private let userDefaults: UserDefaults
+    private let assertionCreator: AssertionCreator
+
     private var assertionID: IOPMAssertionID = 0
     private var hasAssertion = false
     /// Set true while we're rewriting `isEnabled` from inside `applyEnabled` to
     /// revert a failed acquire — the recursive `didSet` reads this and bails.
     private var suppressApply = false

diff --git a/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift b/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..6399c57b0
--- /dev/null
+++ b/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift
@@ -0,0 +1,83 @@
+import IOKit.pwr_mgt
+import XCTest
+@testable import Phoenix
+
+@MainActor
+final class KeepMacAwakeTests: XCTestCase {
+    private var defaults: CountingUserDefaults!
+    private var suiteName: String!
+
+    override func setUp() {
+        super.setUp()
+        suiteName = "KeepMacAwakeTests-\(UUID().uuidString)"
+        defaults = CountingUserDefaults(suiteName: suiteName)!
+        defaults.removePersistentDomain(forName: suiteName)
+    }
+
+    override func tearDown() {
+        defaults.removePersistentDomain(forName: suiteName)
+        defaults = nil
+        suiteName = nil
+        super.tearDown()
+    }
+
+    // Toggle ON → IOPMAssertionCreateWithName fails → isEnabled must revert to
+    // false, UserDefaults must persist false, acquire must be tried exactly
+    // once (no recursion through suppressApply), and exactly one UserDefaults
+    // write of the final value must occur for the toggle interaction.
+    func testFailedAcquireRevertsToggleAndWritesOnceWithoutRecursion() {
+        defaults.set(false, forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+
+        var acquireCallCount = 0
+        let failingAcquire: KeepMacAwake.AssertionCreator = {
+            acquireCallCount += 1
+            return (kIOReturnError, IOPMAssertionID(0))
+        }
+
+        let keepMacAwake = KeepMacAwake(userDefaults: defaults, assertionCreator: failingAcquire)
+        // After init: isEnabled=false, applyEnabled goes to else branch
+        // (releaseAssertion no-op) and writes false to UserDefaults. Reset
+        // the test counters AFTER init to scope the assertions to the
+        // toggle interaction.
+        XCTAssertFalse(keepMacAwake.isEnabled)
+        XCTAssertEqual(acquireCallCount, 0, "init should not attempt acquire when seeded false")
+        defaults.resetWriteCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+
+        // The pre-fix code would have left isEnabled=true and UserDefaults=true
+        // after this assignment because the failure path silently swallowed
+        // the IOReturn. The post-fix code reverts both, in a single bounded
+        // didSet round.
+        keepMacAwake.isEnabled = true
+
+        XCTAssertFalse(keepMacAwake.isEnabled, "isEnabled must revert to false when acquire fails")
+        XCTAssertFalse(
+            defaults.bool(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey),
+            "UserDefaults must persist the reverted false, not the user's intended true"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            acquireCallCount, 1,
+            "acquireAssertion must be called exactly once — suppressApply gate must prevent the inner isEnabled=false write from re-entering applyEnabled and re-invoking the closure"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            defaults.writeCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey), 1,
+            "exactly one UserDefaults write of the resolved value (false), not one per didSet pass"
+        )
+    }
+}
+
+/// Counts `set(_:forKey:)` invocations per key. Subclasses `UserDefaults` so
+/// it slots into `KeepMacAwake`'s injected dependency without a wrapper
+/// protocol; only the Bool overload is overridden because that's the one
+/// `KeepMacAwake.applyEnabled` calls.
+private final class CountingUserDefaults: UserDefaults {
+    private var counts: [String: Int] = [:]
+
+    override func set(_ value: Bool, forKey defaultName: String) {
+        counts[defaultName, default: 0] += 1
+        super.set(value, forKey: defaultName)
+    }
+
+    func writeCount(forKey key: String) -> Int { counts[key] ?? 0 }
+
+    func resetWriteCount(forKey key: String) { counts[key] = 0 }
+}

From d4462c22ee48785f2c9dcdb14733c8b34eef38f5 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Thu, 30 Apr 2026 18:33:26 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 10/12] fix(phoenix): renumber KeepMacAwakeTests pbxproj IDs to
 avoid main collision
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

The IDs FC00/FC01 I used for KeepMacAwakeTests collide with main's
PortAllocator.swift (introduced in #552, container feature). On a fresh
PR-merge build (CI's setup), the merged pbxproj has the same fileRef
ID assigned to two different paths, which Xcode 16.4 reports as:

  warning: file reference for "KeepMacAwakeTests.swift" is a member of
  multiple groups ("Phoenix" and "PhoenixTests"); this indicates a
  malformed project.

The Phoenix target's swift-frontend invocation then includes
KeepMacAwakeTests.swift in its source list, which fails with
"no such module 'XCTest'" because the main app target doesn't link
XCTest.

Local builds didn't trip this because my branch lacks main's container
files. Renumber to FF00/FF01 — verified unused in origin/main.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

From 985517d998d09a2a5b7dc3a2fb22d5eba742fea7 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Fri, 1 May 2026 09:48:43 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 11/12] test(phoenix): cover KeepMacAwake init-path
 failed-acquire revert

The existing failed-acquire test seeds UserDefaults=false and only
exercises the toggle-ON path; init's explicit applyEnabled() call (which
runs when UserDefaults seeded true and acquire fails on launch) is not
covered. testFailedInitFromSeededTrueRevertsAndPersistsFalse seeds true,
injects a failing assertion creator, and asserts isEnabled reverts to
false, UserDefaults persists false, the creator is called exactly once,
and exactly one set(_:Bool, forKey:) write occurs during init.

If the init's applyEnabled() call were removed, the prior test would
still pass; this one catches that regression.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
---
 app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift | 34 ++++++++++++++++++++++++
 1 file changed, 34 insertions(+)

+++ b/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift
@@ -63,6 +63,40 @@ final class KeepMacAwakeTests: XCTestCase {
             "exactly one UserDefaults write of the resolved value (false), not one per didSet pass"
         )
     }
+
+    // Init-path coverage. The toggle test above never exercises init's
+    // explicit applyEnabled() call — if init's revert path were deleted, that
+    // test would still pass. This one seeds UserDefaults=true so init's
+    // applyEnabled is the *only* code path that runs.
+    func testFailedInitFromSeededTrueRevertsAndPersistsFalse() {
+        defaults.set(true, forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+        defaults.resetWriteCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+
+        var acquireCallCount = 0
+        let failingAcquire: KeepMacAwake.AssertionCreator = {
+            acquireCallCount += 1
+            return (kIOReturnError, IOPMAssertionID(0))
+        }
+
+        let keepMacAwake = KeepMacAwake(userDefaults: defaults, assertionCreator: failingAcquire)
+
+        XCTAssertFalse(
+            keepMacAwake.isEnabled,
+            "init must revert isEnabled to false when the seeded-true UserDefaults causes acquire to be attempted and fail"
+        )
+        XCTAssertFalse(
+            defaults.bool(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey),
+            "init must persist the reverted false to UserDefaults so the next launch reads false"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            acquireCallCount, 1,
+            "init's applyEnabled must call the assertion creator exactly once — not twice (recursion) and not zero (skipped init revert)"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            defaults.writeCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey), 1,
+            "init's applyEnabled must write the final value exactly once"
+        )
+    }
 }

From 4d8d34c60b2aa47e81d9b129979fffdaf4cf4a91 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Swagat Patel <swagatpatel@users.noreply.github.com>
Date: Fri, 1 May 2026 09:48:51 -0700
Subject: [PATCH 12/12] fix(phoenix): tighten Keep Mac Awake copy + add
 accuracy tooltip
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

The previous subtitle "Prevent system sleep so your agent stays
reachable" overpromised: kIOPMAssertionTypePreventSystemSleep doesn't
beat clamshell sleep, and on battery the kernel downgrades it to
PreventUserIdleSystemSleep — display sleep still happens.

Subtitle now reads "Prevent idle sleep so your agent stays reachable"
which matches the actual contract. A SwiftUI .help(...) tooltip on the
row spells out the constraints in layperson terms: lid open, plugged
in, battery downgrades to display-on idle-sleep prevention, and lid
close sleeps regardless.

diff --git a/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift b/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift

--- a/app/Phoenix/KeepMacAwake.swift

@@ -10,32 +10,87 @@ import Observation
 /// Clamshell sleep on a MacBook without an external display is still enforced
 /// by the kernel and isn't reachable from a public API.
 @Observable
+@MainActor
 final class KeepMacAwake {
     static let preferencesKey = "keepMacAwakeWhileRunning"
 
+    /// Wraps `IOPMAssertionCreateWithName` so tests can inject deterministic
+    /// failure (kIOReturnError) without depending on real IOKit. The default
+    /// invokes the system call; tests pass a closure that returns whatever
+    /// (IOReturn, IOPMAssertionID) tuple they want.
+    typealias AssertionCreator = () -> (result: IOReturn, assertionID: IOPMAssertionID)
+
+    private let userDefaults: UserDefaults
+    private let assertionCreator: AssertionCreator
+
     private var assertionID: IOPMAssertionID = 0
     private var hasAssertion = false
+    /// Set true while we're rewriting `isEnabled` from inside `applyEnabled` to
+    /// revert a failed acquire — the recursive `didSet` reads this and bails.
+    private var suppressApply = false
 
     var isEnabled: Bool {
-        get { UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: Self.preferencesKey) }
-        set {
-            UserDefaults.standard.set(newValue, forKey: Self.preferencesKey)
-            if newValue {
-                acquireAssertion()
-            } else {
-                releaseAssertion()
+        didSet { applyEnabled() }
+    }
+
+    init(
+        userDefaults: UserDefaults = .standard,
+        assertionCreator: AssertionCreator? = nil
+    ) {
+        self.userDefaults = userDefaults
+        self.assertionCreator = assertionCreator ?? KeepMacAwake.defaultAssertionCreator
+        self.isEnabled = userDefaults.bool(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+        // didSet doesn't fire during init — explicitly run applyEnabled so the
+        // assertion is acquired (and the toggle reverted on failure) on launch
+        // when UserDefaults seeded `isEnabled = true`.
+        applyEnabled()
+    }
+
+    /// Releases the assertion deterministically before the app exits, so the
+    /// system can coalesce any pending sleep state at logout/shutdown without
+    /// waiting for process reaping. The kernel reclaims the assertion on
+    /// process exit either way; this is a courtesy, not a leak fix.
+    func teardown() {
+        releaseAssertion()
+    }
+
+    /// Brings the held IOPMAssertion in line with `isEnabled` and persists the
+    /// resolved value. If acquire fails, `isEnabled` is reverted to false so
+    /// the toggle UI matches the actual sleep-prevention state.
+    private func applyEnabled() {
+        guard !suppressApply else { return }
+        if isEnabled {
+            if !acquireAssertion() {
+                suppressApply = true
+                isEnabled = false
+                suppressApply = false
             }
+        } else {
+            releaseAssertion()
         }
+        userDefaults.set(isEnabled, forKey: Self.preferencesKey)
     }
 
-    init() {
-        if isEnabled {
-            acquireAssertion()
+    private func acquireAssertion() -> Bool {
+        guard !hasAssertion else { return true }
+        let outcome = assertionCreator()
+        guard outcome.result == kIOReturnSuccess else {
+            NSLog("[KeepMacAwake] IOPMAssertionCreateWithName failed: %d", outcome.result)
+            return false
         }
+        assertionID = outcome.assertionID
+        hasAssertion = true
+        return true
+    }
+
+    private func releaseAssertion() {
+        guard hasAssertion else { return }
+        IOPMAssertionRelease(assertionID)
+        hasAssertion = false
+        assertionID = 0
     }
 
-    private func acquireAssertion() {
-        guard !hasAssertion else { return }
+    private static let defaultAssertionCreator: AssertionCreator = {
         var id: IOPMAssertionID = 0
         let reason = "Plow keeping Mac awake so the OpenClaw VM stays reachable" as CFString
         let result = IOPMAssertionCreateWithName(
@@ -44,15 +99,6 @@ final class KeepMacAwake {
             reason,
             &id
         )
-        guard result == kIOReturnSuccess else { return }
-        assertionID = id
-        hasAssertion = true
-    }
-
-    private func releaseAssertion() {
-        guard hasAssertion else { return }

     }
 }

diff --git a/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift b/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..5168019ce
--- /dev/null
+++ b/app/PhoenixTests/KeepMacAwakeTests.swift
@@ -0,0 +1,117 @@
+import IOKit.pwr_mgt
+import XCTest
+@testable import Phoenix
+
+@MainActor
+final class KeepMacAwakeTests: XCTestCase {
+    private var defaults: CountingUserDefaults!
+    private var suiteName: String!
+
+    override func setUp() {
+        super.setUp()
+        suiteName = "KeepMacAwakeTests-\(UUID().uuidString)"
+        defaults = CountingUserDefaults(suiteName: suiteName)!
+        defaults.removePersistentDomain(forName: suiteName)
+    }
+
+    override func tearDown() {
+        defaults.removePersistentDomain(forName: suiteName)
+        defaults = nil
+        suiteName = nil
+        super.tearDown()
+    }
+
+    // Toggle ON → IOPMAssertionCreateWithName fails → isEnabled must revert to
+    // false, UserDefaults must persist false, acquire must be tried exactly
+    // once (no recursion through suppressApply), and exactly one UserDefaults
+    // write of the final value must occur for the toggle interaction.
+    func testFailedAcquireRevertsToggleAndWritesOnceWithoutRecursion() {
+        defaults.set(false, forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+
+        var acquireCallCount = 0
+        let failingAcquire: KeepMacAwake.AssertionCreator = {
+            acquireCallCount += 1
+            return (kIOReturnError, IOPMAssertionID(0))
+        }
+
+        let keepMacAwake = KeepMacAwake(userDefaults: defaults, assertionCreator: failingAcquire)
+        // After init: isEnabled=false, applyEnabled goes to else branch
+        // (releaseAssertion no-op) and writes false to UserDefaults. Reset
+        // the test counters AFTER init to scope the assertions to the
+        // toggle interaction.
+        XCTAssertFalse(keepMacAwake.isEnabled)
+        XCTAssertEqual(acquireCallCount, 0, "init should not attempt acquire when seeded false")
+        defaults.resetWriteCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+
+        // The pre-fix code would have left isEnabled=true and UserDefaults=true
+        // after this assignment because the failure path silently swallowed
+        // the IOReturn. The post-fix code reverts both, in a single bounded
+        // didSet round.
+        keepMacAwake.isEnabled = true
+
+        XCTAssertFalse(keepMacAwake.isEnabled, "isEnabled must revert to false when acquire fails")
+        XCTAssertFalse(
+            defaults.bool(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey),
+            "UserDefaults must persist the reverted false, not the user's intended true"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            acquireCallCount, 1,
+            "acquireAssertion must be called exactly once — suppressApply gate must prevent the inner isEnabled=false write from re-entering applyEnabled and re-invoking the closure"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            defaults.writeCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey), 1,
+            "exactly one UserDefaults write of the resolved value (false), not one per didSet pass"
+        )
+    }
+
+    // Init-path coverage. The toggle test above never exercises init's
+    // explicit applyEnabled() call — if init's revert path were deleted, that
+    // test would still pass. This one seeds UserDefaults=true so init's
+    // applyEnabled is the *only* code path that runs.
+    func testFailedInitFromSeededTrueRevertsAndPersistsFalse() {
+        defaults.set(true, forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+        defaults.resetWriteCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey)
+
+        var acquireCallCount = 0
+        let failingAcquire: KeepMacAwake.AssertionCreator = {
+            acquireCallCount += 1
+            return (kIOReturnError, IOPMAssertionID(0))
+        }
+
+        let keepMacAwake = KeepMacAwake(userDefaults: defaults, assertionCreator: failingAcquire)
+
+        XCTAssertFalse(
+            keepMacAwake.isEnabled,
+            "init must revert isEnabled to false when the seeded-true UserDefaults causes acquire to be attempted and fail"
+        )
+        XCTAssertFalse(
+            defaults.bool(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey),
+            "init must persist the reverted false to UserDefaults so the next launch reads false"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            acquireCallCount, 1,
+            "init's applyEnabled must call the assertion creator exactly once — not twice (recursion) and not zero (skipped init revert)"
+        )
+        XCTAssertEqual(
+            defaults.writeCount(forKey: KeepMacAwake.preferencesKey), 1,
+            "init's applyEnabled must write the final value exactly once"
+        )
+    }
+}
+
+/// Counts `set(_:forKey:)` invocations per key. Subclasses `UserDefaults` so
+/// it slots into `KeepMacAwake`'s injected dependency without a wrapper
+/// protocol; only the Bool overload is overridden because that's the one
+/// `KeepMacAwake.applyEnabled` calls.
+private final class CountingUserDefaults: UserDefaults {
+    private var counts: [String: Int] = [:]
+
+    override func set(_ value: Bool, forKey defaultName: String) {
+        counts[defaultName, default: 0] += 1
+        super.set(value, forKey: defaultName)
+    }
+
+    func writeCount(forKey key: String) -> Int { counts[key] ?? 0 }
+
+    func resetWriteCount(forKey key: String) { counts[key] = 0 }
+}

codex
## [architecture] findings

### Surveyed
- App-wide `KeepMacAwake` ownership in `PhoenixAppDelegate` — clean; matches the current Swift app + plowd host split rather than introducing a daemon/runtime dependency.
- IOKit power assertion wrapper and persistent `UserDefaults` state — clean; no new external dependency or hard-to-reverse data shape.
- Constructor threading through `InstallerView`, `SettingsView`, `MenuBarController`, and `StatusView` — clean; single shared model, no parallel wake-state sources.
- UI placement in installer Settings and connected status popover — clean; the feature is exposed where users already manage local app/runtime controls.
- Current PR diff versus earlier auto-sizing commits — clean; the unrelated installer window sizing path is not present in the live PR diff.
- Phoenix/OpenClaw architecture docs and roadmap boundaries — clean; the change stays in the macOS app layer and does not cross gateway, plowd policy, or VM management boundaries.

## [architecture] findings

### Surveyed
- App-wide `KeepMacAwake` ownership in `PhoenixAppDelegate` — clean; matches the current Swift app + plowd host split rather than introducing a daemon/runtime dependency.
- IOKit power assertion wrapper and persistent `UserDefaults` state — clean; no new external dependency or hard-to-reverse data shape.
- Constructor threading through `InstallerView`, `SettingsView`, `MenuBarController`, and `StatusView` — clean; single shared model, no parallel wake-state sources.
- UI placement in installer Settings and connected status popover — clean; the feature is exposed where users already manage local app/runtime controls.
- Current PR diff versus earlier auto-sizing commits — clean; the unrelated installer window sizing path is not present in the live PR diff.
- Phoenix/OpenClaw architecture docs and roadmap boundaries — clean; the change stays in the macOS app layer and does not cross gateway, plowd policy, or VM management boundaries.
